sentences.text: No.
After the Germans came in my father didn't have any business anymore.
No.
Everything what we had and we were able to save was in the house and we were able only to sell the merchandise little by little to have for food.
And this was the only way how we were able to support ourself.
The business was destroyed.
He was, yes.
He was--
My little brother was too young in the beginning, the first year, to go to work.
But later on he was a substitute in the so-called labor battalion where somebody was supposed to go out and work for the Germans.
If the person had money he used to take a substitute.
So that they were young people going for this kind of work and getting a little bit of money.
This what he did.
My sister was working, as I said, in a public kitchen.
And I was a cashier in a small co-op.
This last till the ghetto was created.
We lived on the outskirt of Warsaw in the suburb Praga.
And when the ghetto was created before the establishment of the ghetto, we have to change and to move out from the so-called Aryan side to the Jewish section.
And this was a very difficult time because we couldn't get an exchange of apartment.
Usually the Poles who lived in the previous assigned to Jewish street, they were looking for better apartment, for larger apartment, and they found it very, very easy.
And it was very difficult for us in the beginning and we were quite concerned.
We didn't know what will happen if we don't get.
But at the last moment we got something, a very bad apartment.
The water was coming on the walls down.
And it was one, two-room apartment, really.
And but we move in this small apartment at that time.
And the ghetto was sealed off in 1940, in October.
If I don't make a mistake, the 16th of October.
And at that time really life changed for the worst.
Oh, there was the law.
There were assignments and there were bulletins, the German in the newspaper.
So all the Jews knew right away that this and this district, this number of street is being assigned for the ghetto, and all who live beyond have to move in there.
There was a few weeks' time we have to find an exchange, an apartment.
And at that time I remember whatever we were able to take with us on a very small van, and we were schlepping whatever we were able to to this small apartment there on [?
Leshner ?] 66, which was assigned for the Jews.
This was really the critical point in our life when things started to slide down.
Father got sick.
He got pneumonia.
We couldn't save him.
It was no antibiotic in the ghetto and he died.
And mother and sister and brother remained in the-- with me.
And--
47 years.
And whatever we were able to sell, still little things, we were selling.
We were selling to smugglers, to Polish smugglers which used to come into the ghetto.
Bring some food and exchange, buying all kinds of stuff that Jews had to sell.
And--
My sister was in the ghetto working on her job.
She was taken late though when deportation started from her job.
My little brother was at home, sometimes going out on the labor battalions, but not too often.
And I was also working, as I said, in the ghetto, on the job.

But at the same time, in the ghetto I was involved with the illegal youth organization, the social democratic youth organization called And we used to come together in little groups having all kind of lectures, listening to even to music sometimes, having a seminar.
But these were small groups.
Reading illegal literature.
And this was, in a way, a little bit of an involvement which my mother, although she was not too much anxious for us to do this kind of work knowing that if German find out then death.
It was no school at that time in the ghetto.

And we, the young people, started to be involved in teaching the children in the so-called-- we called this the children corners where children who were not able to go to school-- the schools were closed-- we used to take them to ghetto to teach them songs, to teach them how to write and, in a way, giving them a little bit of life.
And in the houses there they were created house committees to help the starvation which started in the ghetto.
In the ghetto, in the Warsaw ghetto itself, over 2,000 house committees were created by the Jews themselves, the so-called [INAUDIBLE].
Illegal Jewish cultural organization was created.
And we, the young people, started to have seminars, I recall, that and later assigned to places to go after the curfew and to have lectures for the people of the houses which were closed up.
So I, a 16, 17-year-old, went to such lecture and I recall it was [INAUDIBLE] and I was talking about [INAUDIBLE], about [NON-ENGLISH] at this particular lecture.
And two young people, children, were outside the windows, were covered, in case German will come and knock to have time that we will be able to disperse.
And I slept over at that time in one of the apartments of the people.

It's very difficult for me to say how the lecture, what they talk, but I still remember the atmosphere, the uplift that in the ghetto with so much starvation and the typhoid epidemic which started and hunger and misery, we were talking about literature.
And a young girl was talking to older people and they were listening.
And somehow it was-- they thought, they hoped that this will pass.
It is a time that will not remain forever.
And this kind of hope was constantly in the life of the ghetto.
My mother was really-- today, as a mother by myself I look back and whenever I mentioned her name I was wondering how she was able to manage.
It was a poor household, really.
And, nevertheless, somehow, she prepared food and meal for the children.
She sold-- start selling-- we didn't have already merchandise anymore from the house.
The most valuable things to be able to prepare something.

She by herself didn't eat so much, and quite often I remind she want very much that my little brother should prepare for bar mitzvah and didn't have any possibility to pay the teacher, the religious teacher, with money.
So she was cutting away her portion of bread and hiding it for us children, and this was the payment for the [INAUDIBLE].
We knew where she was hiding.
In bed.
But none of us, although we were very hungry, none of us really was thinking about taking away this.
And I remember my mother with poofed up from hunger under the eyes and hiding the bread, her portion, to prepare my little brother for the bar mitzvah which he never had.
They were deported.
So how my mother managed, difficult to say how she kept the house clean without soap, without hot water, but she did.
She tried.
And my mother was not the only one in the ghetto.
This were the mothers trying to keep and to hold on, hoping that it will come to an end.
That somehow we will survive.
Did your sister participate in the same youth organizations?
My sister was proud, but didn't active participate.
She was more the speaking Polish, having her younger friends, and was not too much involved, I would say.
She was also more busy.
I was more active.
I was more active at that time.
Always a more active person in the organizational, cultural involvement.
But when sometimes I used to come home with literature, illegal, and I give it to my mother and I thought it's-- you have to hide it.
She didn't ask.
She didn't say anything.
She put it away somewhere.
And then I turned to her a day, two, three, where is it?
I need it now.
She gave it to me.
So she was indirectly, indirectly involved in my illegal war in the ghetto.
Simple woman, my mother.
But one of the mothers-- [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] which very little is being talking about it.
We talk about uprising.
We talk about resistance.
We talk about things which is for the eye and for action.
But about this simple, quiet, and dedicated souls we give very little attention.
And I think history has to see them a little bit more sharp, as they were.
Why do you think we don't pay more attention to these mothers?
We don't-- why I think-- our time is so different from the time that I lived in the ghetto.
Time is now most is taken with action.
Let's say resistance is understood if you have a gun, if you stand up with a knife, if you do something.

The resistance in different forms which was expressed in the time of the occupation in the ghetto was very often a different way of resistance which has to be understood if you go back to these particular years.
It was not the gun and the revolt.

But it was the inner strength, it was the tradition, the morality, the ethic which our mothers lived and our generations before us lived, and it was expressed in simple, little things, in 1,000 instances of resistance about we take it for granted.
We don't mention this.
But at that time, it was more than little things.
It was strength, which has to be understood.

I would say that later on when I got involved more in the illegal work and we start thinking and when deportation were held, about deportation in other ghettos, in other towns, and most of the people who were in the youth underground, the organized, not in the hundreds of hundreds, but a very small group.
They were, in a way, the foundation of the later organized, armed resistance which took place.
But when I look back, I think that the armed resistance was possible because it was an ethical, moral resistance going on in the ghetto before deportation.
It is a chain.
It didn't come suddenly.
The young people came from the houses of the parents who were transported to the gas chambers.
But the houses were full of beliefs, of hope, of belief in humanity, of justice, and this was a chain leading to active resistance.
And this is why I cannot separate the uprising or active resistance or the partizan resistance from the resistance which took place in the quiet, so-called times in the ghetto.
This was the base, the foundation.
And the foundation goes even before the war, the outbreak of the war.
So it was a chain of resistance in our Jewish life, which culmination was the armed resistance.
It started-- my armed resistance-- I would not say armed resistance.
I would say resistance was all the time in the ghetto, before the ghetto.
My involvement in preparation for the uprising started from these small, little groups.
And this were the groups from all kind of political youth movements.
Hashomer Hatzair, the Bund, the communists, the-- the other.
Habonim.
They created later on when deportation was going on and with-- in the beginning, we didn't know that it really deportation meant death.
I remember when some streets were blocked, blockades were set, and they started to take the Jews out.
We thought at that time that the Jews in the beginning are going to other cities.
Just resettlement to the east.
In the Warsaw ghetto we saw resettlement from the west.
Jews were coming from the German part.
Jews were coming from the north, and they were coming to the Warsaw ghetto.
So we understood that now, probably, deportation will go to other cities.
And my mother was very much concerned that if she will be caught and deported she will be by herself.
And she broke her leg in the beginning of the war, which was constantly bothering her.
And the father was no more.
He was dead.
And constantly talking to us.
What will happen in another city if I am by myself?
And I used to quiet her down that we will never be separated.
We will be always together.
You will never be by yourself.
But destiny wanted different.
Was-- everything which develop was in a different direction.
When did you first realize that people from the west were brought in to the ghetto?
When they started coming in.
German speaking from Poznan, from other city, they started come into the Warsaw ghetto.
And we have to find location.
I mean, the ghetto.
The council has to find location to-- and they were called the refugee points.
And then-- we saw them.
So it's not a question of realization.
We came across.
We met them every day.
Before the deportations started.
They started to come from the west.
From-- from [?
Poland, ?] yes.
From Poland.
From the west side of Poland, yes.
Some from Germany too.
Some from Germany, from certain-- we realize it, but at that time was not deportation to the east, to gas chambers.
[INAUDIBLE] was the first deported German Jews who came to Poland not being German citizen-- with Polish citizenship.
But at that time we didn't think-- the war didn't break out at the time and it was not deportation.
So, but this was realization, that deportation can take place.
But we didn't think about it.
We didn't think in the beginning of deportation, that people are really going to be gassed.
We in the underground heard that, for instance, in [NON-ENGLISH] there are vents where people are being gassed, but we didn't realize it.
We thought groups of people.
Even I didn't realize that it really is that people-- whole cities and towns are being wiped out.
I thought that it is in camps, in the beginning, in settlements in other ghettos, in other cities.
And this was the main thought of the majority of the people in the Warsaw ghetto.
In-- in the Warsaw ghetto people also thought that Warsaw is the capitol and it's open for the whole world and there are embassies.
And how can they do even the same thing in such a big city with so many Jews, a half a million Jews in the ghetto?
What they are doing in a small town?
So people didn't believe that it's really going, being deported to nowhere, or being killed.
Who was the underground leadership at the time?
The leadership in the ghetto was the Jewish council, the Judenrat.
But the underground leadership was in the hand of the young people, as I said, of the youth organization.
And they formed the so-called fighter's organization.
And this was in 1942.
Deportation started the 22nd of July, 1942.
And then representative of youth organizations got together and they-- something has to be done.
And, really, I would say in October, 1942, the organization came to life.
An understanding came that some actions in preparing the youth if another deportation starts that their response should be in an organized way.
We didn't have any arms.
I belonged to the first group of the so-called fighter's organization.
We didn't have any arms at that time.
It was a need to get arm.
It was a need to prepare ourself.
And in December, 1942, they turned to me-- it was the Bund, the third group.

That because I look like a Polish girl, there are a few others on the other side of the wall-- on the other side of the ghetto wall-- to get out and to walk in the underground on the Polish side to try to prepare, to buy on the black market guns.
Whatever is possible.
And also to prepare some hiding places for children, if it's possible there.
To find out.
And this is how I got out of the ghetto.
And I recall it was a big meeting in November, 1942, from the youth group of the Bund.

And they were talking at that time about the organization which was formed, the Jewish fighter's organization, and the involvement and our responsibility that when the Germans will come to take us out from the shops, to stand up, not to go.
Whatever it is, to give a resistance.
And at that time each of us have got some assignment.
I didn't get any assignment, and I felt very low that the only person-- it was a group of 28 or 29 people, young people-- and I felt really bad.
But when everybody already start to disperse, their leader was at that time of this group, Abrasza Blum, came over to me and told me that your assignment will be different.
That you will not remain in the ghetto.
Somebody will come and give you some information later on.
And you will get out of the ghetto.
The assignment will be on the other side of the ghetto to do the utmost for the fighters organization.
And this was my assignment, how I got involved in the fighters organization.
Abrasza Blum was the leader of the Jewish Bund group.
And he was the one also who was responsible together with the young people there-- Marek Edelman, who is now in Warsaw still alive, Dr.
Edelman, and Michal Klepfisz-- responsible for organizing the four Bund youth groups in the fighter's organization.
They were also the representative together and meet with Mordechaj Anielewicz from Hashomer Hatzair-- they had four groups.
They meet with representative from other youth groups.
And this is how they started to work to coordinate.
The first really response of the organized armed resistance took place the 18th of January, 1943.
But this was not too much organized yet.
And I would not say that it was very successful.
But this was the first attempt.
And after it they came to the conclusion that they have to separate the youth groups.
They have to live constantly together to be ready in case of need to be in an organized way to step and to stand up.
And mine assignments were, if I could get and I got ones, a few kilo of dynamite to smuggle this into the ghetto.
Usually I had a code number by telephone from the working shops in the ghetto and from other youth which was staying near the telephone.
And we used to give our code and to decide at what point we will smuggle over dynamite or guns or things.
And this is how I got more and more involved.
I used to come in into the ghetto as a smuggler and bringing in something like bottle or something, different things.
But the bottle was just the paper and inside was the dynamite.
And to smuggle out sometimes jewelry to change it there.
By this time the underground knew more about gas chambers, yes.
When they ask me, for instance, to get out of the ghetto, at this time I had to smuggle out a map of Treblinka, which they already knew how and where it is created and formed.
And I had the first account.
Very, very difficult but also lucky.
At this particular time when I got the assignment is for the first time I got to know mine future husband.
He was working on the Polish side in a Jewish labor battalion and he was also able sometimes somebody has to get out of the ghetto to bribe the group leader, Ukrainian.

And the underground told me that I have to get in contact with him where he lives and he will include me in the labor group-- of course, bribing the group leader, the Ukrainian-- and to get out this way.
And this is how I got to know him the first time.
Later on I met him more often on the inside.
At that time I had the map of the Treblinka in my shoes.
And having in my shoes, I stood with all the other people in the group.
And I was one woman among men, only men, and they singled me out and they asked me to go to the checking point.
What I have?
They understood that I have some smuggle or something, some clothing, some special thing.
And I went into the shack where the German was checking me, and it was a German there.
At the place to go out of the ghetto was Polish police and Jewish police and German.
And he asked me to take off the clothes and everything.
And I was thinking at that time, if you only would not ask me to take off the shoes but then he will find and I am done.
And this particular moment the door of the shack got opened and they called him up that somebody run away.
And he went out.
And I on mine way started to put back everything and got back the rest.
And when I was dressed, I went out and I told the Polish police I was checked and I went back to the group.
And the group went out of to ghetto.
